---
title: "Matrices"
description: "The matrix of a linear map, rank normal form, and matrix addition and multiplication from the linear-map viewpoint — an augmented summary of Axler's LADR §3.C."
date: "2026-06-05"
date-modified: "2026-06-19"
categories: ["mathematics", "linear-algebra", "ladr"]
execute:
    echo: true
---

{{< include ../../_macros.qmd >}}

This is an augmented summary of Section 3.C of [Linear Algebra Done Right](https://linear.axler.net/index.html) by Sheldon Axler.

As in the previous section, we assume that $\mb{F}\in\{\mathbb{R},\mathbb{C}\}$.

## Matrices
Consider finite-dimensional vector spaces $V$ and $W$, and let $T$ be a linear map from $V$ to $W$. For bases $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ and $w_1,\cdots,w_m$ of $V$ and $W$ respectively, there exist $m\times n$ scalars $A_{i,j}$ such that

$$
Tv_j=A_{1,j}w_1+\cdots+A_{m,j}w_m.
$$

That is, we have

- first index = row number = $i$th coordinate in $W$
- second index = column number = $j$th basis in $V$.

We can collect these scalars into an $m\times n$ **matrix** of the form

$$
\mathcal{M}(T)=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

This matrix represents $T$ *with respect to* this pair of basis. The number of rows (vertical height) of this matrix corresponds to the dimension of the codomain $W$, while the number of columns (horizontal width) corresponds to the dimension of the domain $V$.

<details class="example">
<summary>Matrix of linear map between $\mb{F}^n$</summary>

Let $T\in\mathcal{L}(\mb{F}^2,\mb{F}^3)$ be given by

$$T(x,y)=(2x-y,-3x+2y,5x-y).$$

Then we have $T(1,0)=(2,-3,5)$ and $T(0,1)=(-1,2,-1)$, and so the matrix of $T$ with respect to the standard bases of $\mb{F}^2$ and $\mb{F}^3$ is

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
2&-1\\
-3&2\\
5&-1
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

</details>

<details class="example">
<summary>Matrix of differentiation map</summary>

Consider the differentiation map $D:\mathcal{P}_3(\mathbb{R})\to\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})$. We wish to find the corresponding matrix with respect to the standard bases for $\mathcal{P}_3(\mathbb{R})$ and $\mathcal{P}_2(\mathbb{R})$. 

As the $n$th standard basis vector $x^{n-1}$ maps to $(n-1)x^{n-2}$ under $D$, the $n$th column (tracking how the $n$th basis vector is transformed by $D$) will contain $n-1$ at the $(n-1)$th entry. So the matrix is

$$
\mathcal{M}(D)=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1&0&0\\
0&0&2&0\\
0&0&0&3
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

</details>

Since we already know what matrix multiplication looks like from other sources: Despite the equation $Tv_j=\sum_{i=1}^mA_{i,j}w_i$ above, we *should not* have in our minds

$$
\begin{pmatrix}
Tv_1\\
Tv_2\\
\vdots\\
Tv_n
\end{pmatrix}=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
w_1\\
w_2\\
\vdots\\
w_m
\end{pmatrix},
$$

which is dimensionally mismatched to begin with. The action of $T$ on the individual basis vectors $v_i$ is expressed as (taking $v_1$ as an example)

$$
\mathcal{M}(Tv_1)=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}\\
A_{2,1}\\
\vdots\\
A_{m,1}
\end{pmatrix}=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
1\\
0\\
\vdots\\
0
\end{pmatrix}=\mathcal{M}(T)\mathcal{M}(v_1),
$$

where $\mathcal{M}(v_1)$ on the right expresses the coordinates of $v_1$ with respect to $v_1,\cdots,v_n$, and $\mathcal{M}(Tv_1)$ on the left expresses the coordinates of $Tv_1$ with respect to $w_1,\cdots,w_m$. (Note that we had not defined the action of $\mathcal{M}$ on vectors above. We will say more about this in the next section.)

Subsequently, the action on an arbitrary vector $a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n\in V$ is given by

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal{M}(T(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n))&=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
a_1\\
0\\
\vdots\\
0
\end{pmatrix}\\
&\quad+\cdots+
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
0\\
0\\
\vdots\\
a_n
\end{pmatrix}
\\
&=
a_1
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}\\
A_{2,1}\\
\vdots\\
A_{m,1}
\end{pmatrix}
+\cdots+
a_n
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,n}\\
\vdots\\
A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}\\
&=a_1\mathcal{M}(T)\mathcal{M}(v_1)+\cdots+a_n\mathcal{M}(T)\mathcal{M}(v_n)\\
&=\mathcal{M}(T)\mathcal{M}(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n),
\end{aligned}
$$

where we have taken for granted some properties regarding $\mathcal{M}$ and matrix operations in the last line: namely, 

- $a\mathcal{M}(X)=\mathcal{M}(aX)$.
- $AB+AC=A(B+C)$.
- $\mathcal{M}(X+Y)=\mathcal{M}(X)+\mathcal{M}(Y)$.

#### Rows and columns
Let $A$ be an $m\times n$ matrix. Then

- $A_{i,\cdot}$ denotes the $1\times n$ matrix (row matrix) consisting of the $i$th row of $A$
- $A_{\cdot,j}$ denotes the $m\times 1$ matrix (column matrix) consisting of the $j$th column of $A$. 

#### Minimum number of non-zero entries
:::{#thm-min-nonzero-entries}
Suppose that $V$ and $W$ are finite-dimensional and $T:V\to W$. Then for any choice of bases for $V$ and $W$, the corresponding matrix for $T$ has at least $\dim\im\ T$ non-zero entries.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Keeping a basis vector alive under $T$ requires a non-zero entry in the matrix of $T$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

The rank-nullity theorem states that

$$\dim\im\ T=\dim V-\dim\ker T.$$

For $\dim V-\dim\ker T$ (the dimension of a kernel complement) to be equal to $d$, we must map $d$ basis vectors to a non-zero vector. As we can see from the definition of a matrix, this requires at least $d$ non-zero entries in the matrix of $T$. 

</details>

###### Rank normal form
:::{#thm-rank-normal-form}
Let $V$ and $W$ be finite-dimensional. There exist bases of $V$ and $W$ with respect to which the matrix of $T$ contains exactly $\dim\im\ T$ non-zero entries, namely $1$ in the first $\dim\im\ T$ diagonal entries.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Consider the pre-image of a basis of $W$.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Let $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ be a basis of $\im\ T$, and let $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ be vectors in $V$ such that $Tv_i=w_i$. Then we know that $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is linearly independent, because $w_1,\cdots,w_n$ is.

In addition, let $v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ be a basis of $\ker T$, where $m+n=\dim V$ by the rank-nullity theorem. Since

$$
\begin{aligned}
&0=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n+b_1v'_1+\cdots+b_mv'_m\\
\implies&0=T(a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n+b_1v'_1+\cdots+b_mv'_m)=b_1w_1+\cdots+b_mw_m\\
\implies&0=b_1=\cdots=b_m\\
\implies&0=a_1=\cdots=a_n,
\end{aligned}
$$

we see that $v_1,\cdots,v_n,v'_1,\cdots,v'_m$ is linearly independent. As this set has the right size, it is a basis of $V$. 

Let $w_1,\cdots,w_n,w'_1,\cdots,w'_{n'}$ be an extension of the $w_i$s to a basis of $W$. Then the matrix of $T$ with respect to

$$v_1,\cdots,v_n,v'_1,\cdots,v'_m\quad\text{and}\quad w_1,\cdots,w_n,w'_1,\cdots,w'_{n'}$$

has the requisite form.

</details>

#### Matrix for rank one linear maps
:::{#thm-rank-one-matrix}
For a linear map $T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)$ between finite-dimensional vector spaces, we have $\dim\im\ T=1$ if and only if there are bases of $V$ and $W$ such that every entry of $\mathcal{M}(T)$ is 1.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
This requires a "division of labour" between the basis of $V$ and the basis of $W$. The former ensures that every basis vector of $V$ is mapped to the same $w\in W$, while the latter resolves this $w$ into $w_1+\cdots+w_m$.

For the first task, kernel vectors can be used to enlarge the basis while keeping the image lean. For the second task, force $w=w_1+\cdots+w_m$ by the definition of the basis.
:::

<details class="proof">
<summary>Full proof</summary>

Suppose that $\dim\im\ T=1$, and let $w$ be a non-zero vector in $\im\ T$. As $w$ spans $\im\ T$, we have

$$Tv_i=\lambda_iw$$

for some $\lambda_1,\cdots,\lambda_n\in\mb{F}$, where $v_1,\cdots,v_n$ is a basis of $V$.

Let $v\in V$ be a non-zero vector not in $\ker T$, which exists because $\dim\ker T=n-1$ by the rank-nullity theorem. We have to extend it to a basis of $V$ while avoiding any vectors in $\ker T$. So let $k_1,\cdots,k_{n-1}$ be a basis of $\ker T$. Then we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
0&=av+a_1(v+k_1)+\cdots+a_{n-1}(v+k_{n-1})\\
&=(a+a_1+\cdots+a_{n-1})v+a_1k_1+\cdots+a_{n-1}k_{n-1}.
\end{aligned}
$$

But this implies that $a_1k_1+\cdots+a_{n-1}k_{n-1}$ is a scalar multiple of $v\in V-\ker T$, which is only possible if that scalar is $0$. The linear independence of the $k_i$s implies $a_1=\cdots=a_{n-1}=0$, which subsequently leads to $a=0$. Therefore, the set

$$v,v+k_1,\cdots,v+k_{n-1}$$

is linearly independent, and is thereby a basis due to its size. Furthermore, we have 

$$T(v+k_i)=Tv\neq0$$

as required.

Now, let $w'_1,\cdots,w'_{m-1}$ be a basis of a complement $U$ of $\im\ T$. Then the set

$$w'_1,\cdots,w'_{m-1},w-(w'_1+\cdots+w'_{m-1})$$

spans $W$, because $W=\im\ T\oplus U$ and the set can express any vector in $\im\ T$ and $U$. As it has the right size, the set is a basis of $W$.

Since we have

$$w=w'_1+\cdots+w'_{m-1}+w-(w'_1+\cdots+w'_{m-1}),$$

this basis satisfies our needs.

So we have found the bases of $V$ and $W$ such that

$$Tv_1=\cdots=Tv_n=w_1+\cdots+w_m.$$

Conversely, suppose that there exist bases such that $\mathcal{M}(T)$ has all-$1$ entries. Then

$$Tv_j=w_1+\cdots+w_m=w$$

for all $j=1,\cdots,n$, and so for an arbitrary $v=a_1v_1+\cdots+a_nv_n$, we find

$$Tv=a_1Tv_1+\cdots+a_nTv_n=(a_1+\cdots+a_n)w.$$

Therefore, $\im\ T$ is spanned by $w$, which means that $\dim\im\ T=1$.

</details>

#### Matrix addition and multiplication
Given two linear maps $S,T\in\mathcal{L}(V,W)$ with

$$
\begin{gather}
Tv_j=a_{1,j}w_1+\cdots+a_{m,j}w_m\\
Sv_j=b_{1,j}w_1+\cdots+b_{m,j}w_m,
\end{gather}
$$

the linear map $T+S$ acts on the basis as

$$
(T+S)v_j=(a_{1,j}+b_{1,j})w_1+\cdots+(a_{m,j}+b_{m,j})w_m.
$$

So the matrix corresponding to $T+S$ has the form

$$
\mathcal{M}(T+S)=
\begin{pmatrix}
a_{1,1}+b_{1,1}&a_{1,2}+b_{1,2}&\cdots&a_{1,n}+b_{1,n}\\
a_{2,1}+b_{2,1}&a_{2,2}+b_{2,2}&\cdots&a_{2,n}+b_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
a_{m,1}+b_{m,1}&a_{m,2}+b_{m,2}&\cdots&a_{m,n}+b_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

Therefore, if we define matrix addition in the element-wise manner as

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}+
\begin{pmatrix}
B_{1,1}&B_{1,2}&\cdots&B_{1,n}\\
B_{2,1}&B_{2,2}&\cdots&B_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
B_{m,1}&B_{m,2}&\cdots&B_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}\\
&\qquad=
\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}+B_{1,1}&A_{1,2}+B_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}+B_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}+B_{2,1}&A_{2,2}+B_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}+B_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}+B_{m,1}&A_{m,2}+B_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}+B_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix},
\end{aligned}
$$

then we have $\mathcal{M}(S+T)=\mathcal{M}(S)+\mathcal{M}(T)$.

By an analogous reasoning, if we define scalar multiplication element-wise as

$$
\lambda\begin{pmatrix}
A_{1,1}&A_{1,2}&\cdots&A_{1,n}\\
A_{2,1}&A_{2,2}&\cdots&A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
A_{m,1}&A_{m,2}&\cdots&A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix}=
\begin{pmatrix}
\lambda A_{1,1}&\lambda A_{1,2}&\cdots&\lambda A_{1,n}\\
\lambda A_{2,1}&\lambda A_{2,2}&\cdots&\lambda A_{2,n}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
\lambda A_{m,1}&\lambda A_{m,2}&\cdots&\lambda A_{m,n}
\end{pmatrix},
$$

then we have $\mathcal{M}(\lambda T)=\lambda\mathcal{M}(T)$.

###### Linear map and vector space
From the above definitions of addition and multiplication, we can conclude two things.

:::{#thm-matrices-vector-space}
First, if we define $\mb{F}^{m,n}$ to be the set of all $m\times n$ matrices, then we can verify that $\mb{F}^{m,n}$ forms a vector space under the operations defined above.
:::

:::{#thm-matrix-map-linear}
Second, $\mathcal{M}$ can be viewed as a linear map from $\mathcal{L}(V,W)$ to $\mb{F}^{m,n}$, since additivity and homogeneity hold by the definitions of the operations.
:::

###### Dimension of $\mb{F}^{m,n}$
:::{#thm-dimension-matrices}
The dimension of $\mb{F}^{m,n}$ is given by $mn$.
:::

:::{.proof-idea}
Consider the $m\times n$ matrix units, which has $1$ in a single entry and $0$ everywhere else.
:::

#### Matrix multiplication
Consider the linear maps $T:U\to V$ and $S:V\to W$, where the dimensions of $U$, $V$ and $W$ are $m$, $n$, and $p$ respectively. Then their product acts on the basis vectors of $U$ as

$$
\begin{aligned}
(ST)u_j&=S(\sum_{i=1}^na_{i,j}v_i)\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^na_{i,j}Sv_i\\
&=\sum_{i=1}^na_{i,j}(\sum_{k=1}^pb_{k,i}w_k).
\end{aligned}
$$

This sum contains all terms of the form $a_{i,j}b_{k,i}w_k$ for $i=1,\cdots,n$ and $k=1,\cdots,p$, and so we may write it as

$$
(ST)u_j=\sum_{k=1}^p(\sum_{i=1}^nb_{k,i}a_{i,j})w_k.
$$

So the matrix corresponding to $ST$ has the form

$$
\mathcal{M}(ST)=
\begin{pmatrix}
\sum_{i=1}^nb_{1,i}a_{i,1}&\sum_{i=1}^nb_{1,i}a_{i,2}&\cdots&\sum_{i=1}^nb_{1,i}a_{i,m}\\
\sum_{i=1}^nb_{2,i}a_{i,1}&\sum_{i=1}^nb_{2,i}a_{i,2}&\cdots&\sum_{i=1}^nb_{2,i}a_{i,m}\\
\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\
\sum_{i=1}^nb_{p,i}a_{i,1}&\sum_{i=1}^nb_{p,i}a_{i,2}&\cdots&\sum_{i=1}^nb_{p,i}a_{i,m}
\end{pmatrix}.
$$

We define this to be the matrix product $\mathcal{M}(S)\mathcal{M}(T)$. Of course, this is identical to what we find through the usual algorithmic computation of $\mathcal{M}(S)\mathcal{M}(T)$.

###### Properties of matrix multiplication
:::{#thm-matrix-mult-properties}
From the definition, it is possible (but tedious) to check that the matrix multiplication satisfies the associativity and distributivity properties. 
:::

However, matrix multiplication is famously non-commutative.

###### Different ways to view matrix multiplication
Let us now think about expressing the matrix product $AB$ (where $A$ and $B$ have shapes $m\times n$ and $n\times p$ respectively).

Each entry at $(i,j)$ is the product of the $i$th row of $A$ and $j$th column of $B$:

$$(AB)_{i,j}=A_{i,\cdot}B_{\cdot, j}.$$

Consequently, the $j$th column of the product is obtained by multiplying each row of $A$ by $B_{\cdot,j}$:

$$
(AB)_{\cdot,j}=AB_{\cdot,j}
$$

For any $n\times 1$ matrix $c$ with entries $c_1,\cdots,c_n$, we have

$$
Ac=
\begin{pmatrix}
\sum_{i=1}^na_{1,i}c_i\\
\vdots\\
\sum_{i=1}^na_{m,i}c_i
\end{pmatrix}=
\begin{pmatrix}
a_{1,1}c_1+\cdots+a_{1,n}c_n\\
\vdots\\
a_{m,1}c_1+\cdots+a_{m,n}c_n
\end{pmatrix}=
c_1A_{\cdot,1}+\cdots+c_nA_{\cdot,n}.
$$
